name: building-plugins
description: Expert at creating and managing Claude Code plugins that bundle agents, skills, commands, and hooks into cohesive packages. Auto-invokes when the user wants to create, structure, validate, or publish a complete plugin, or needs help with plugin architecture and best practices. Also auto-invokes proactively when Claude is about to create plugin directory structures, write plugin.json manifests, or implement tasks that involve bundling components into a plugin package.
capabilities: Creates plugin directory structures with proper organization
Validates plugin structure and component compliance
Generates security validation hooks for dangerous commands
Provides templates for minimal, standard, and full plugins
author: C0ntr0lledCha0s
securitySummary: All 588 static findings evaluated as false positives. The skill is documentation/educational content for building Claude Code plugins. Critical findings involve defensive security validation code (detecting dangerous commands) and security recommendations (avoiding eval). External command references are markdown documentation examples, not executable code.
valueStatement: Create plugins that bundle agents, skills, commands, and hooks into distributable packages. Includes validation tools, templates, and best practices for secure plugin development.
limitations: Does not execute arbitrary code or modify external systems
Does not publish plugins to registries (only prepares them)
Does not access credentials or secrets from outside the plugin
Templates require manual configuration before use

useCases.title: Create distribution-ready plugins
useCases.description: Bundle multiple components into a single installable package with proper manifest and structure.

useCases.title: Standardize team plugin templates
useCases.description: Enforce consistent architecture and naming conventions across team plugins.

useCases.title: Prepare plugins for sharing
useCases.description: Validate plugins follow best practices and include proper documentation.

promptTemplates.title: New plugin structure
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a new plugin named [plugin-name] with a [minimal|standard|full] template

promptTemplates.title: Add components
promptTemplates.prompt: Add a new [skill|command|agent] named [name] to my existing plugin at [path]

promptTemplates.title: Validate structure
promptTemplates.prompt: Validate my plugin at [path] and check for security issues

promptTemplates.title: Generate manifest
promptTemplates.prompt: Generate a complete plugin.json for a [type] plugin with [components]

bestPractices: Use minimal allowed-tools lists and require user confirmation for destructive operations
Store credentials in environment variables and add secrets files to .gitignore
Validate all script inputs and avoid eval() or dynamic code execution
antiPatterns: Hardcoding API keys or credentials in plugin.json or source files
Skipping security validation hooks for Bash tool usage
Publishing plugins without reviewing component permissions

faq.question: What is the difference between a skill and a plugin?
faq.answer: A skill is a single auto-invoked capability. A plugin bundles multiple related skills, commands, agents, and hooks into one distributable package.

faq.question: Can I include secrets in my plugin?
faq.answer: Never hardcode secrets. Use environment variables like ${API_KEY} and document required configuration for users to set up.

faq.question: How do I validate my plugin before publishing?
faq.answer: Run the validate-plugin.py script to check structure, schema compliance, and security patterns. Address any warnings before distribution.

faq.question: What permissions should my plugin request?
faq.answer: Use the minimum required tools. Only request dangerous tools like Bash or Write when necessary and require user confirmation.

faq.question: How do I version my plugin?
faq.answer: Follow semantic versioning (MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH). Bump MAJOR for breaking changes, MINOR for new features, PATCH for fixes.

faq.question: Can I use hooks for security?
faq.answer: Yes. PreToolUse hooks can validate dangerous commands before execution. See the validate-bash.sh hook template for examples.
